item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the concept behind this.. but I have too many issues with it. My -1 won't mean much in the sea of +200, but oh well.

The biggest problem to me is that if this thing is so dangerous in so little time, how was it ever contained? A tale or recovery log could go a long way in explaining this inconsistency.

My second problem is that the main article is so math-heavy, it's near impossible to understand without some kind of math background. Which is… tough to resolve.

The charts are helpful, but I think more tests like in addendum A would help to alleviate the issue. Again, adding a tale or recovery log… Could solve both of the issues I have with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More useful, yet odd science-fiction than the usual creepy faire on here, but I like it…hell I'd run on it for an hour in my time to come out a few decades in the future…heh. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As I recall, one of the Xyank stories directly mentions this SCP, so yeah, I'm cool with a link to the Delta-T's.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a few potential links for this skip. First off, the Temporal Anomalies Department would be all over this skip given its nature, so a mention of them in the containment procedures would make sense in-universe. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1979 is held at Site ███ near ██████████ Bay, Alaska. No other temporally manipulative objects are allowed within 100 kilometers of this site. It is to remain powered at all times, with triple redundancy power supplies. It must be used for a minimum of 55 minutes out of every hour in a darkened, lead-lined room. A mechanic skilled in rapidly repairing models similar to SCP-1979 must be on staff and readily available at all times, in case emergency repairs are needed.

To this:

SCP-1979 is held at Site ███ near ██████████ Bay, Alaska. No other temporally manipulative objects are allowed within 100 kilometers of this site, per order of the Temporal Anomalies Department It is to remain powered at all times, with triple redundancy power supplies. It must be used for a minimum of 55 minutes out of every hour in a darkened, lead-lined room. A mechanic skilled in rapidly repairing models similar to SCP-1979 must be on staff and readily available at all times, in case emergency repairs are needed.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suggest testing with SCP-442

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What amuses me the most about this thing is that the only real danger from the Doom Bubble is the slow rate of expansion. Standing on the edge of the bubble is what kills you; if you're fully inside or fully outside, the only effect is that your observations of the other side start to look really weird.

If the bubble were large enough to cover the solar system, no one would notice that it existed… and the only reason we can't do that is because getting to that point requires the edge to pass through everyone at a uniform rate of 0.5 cm/s. A sufficiently high ratio of dilation between opposite sides of the body will create imbalances that cause heart attacks- but if the expansion rate was more like 1 m/s or 2 m/s, those imbalances might not have time to fully develop, and people might survive.

(As it is, the best chance of getting through a sharp enough drop alive is by throwing yourself at the barrier really fast, so you pass through in as little time as possible…)

Now I'm thinking about how gravity translates from one side of the bubble to the other. If we evacuated a really big area and let the bubble grow, would the ratio of Earth gravity to Sun gravity affecting that region eventually change? Or the direction of those gravitational forces, relative to the position of that part of space (as the Earth turns and revolves around the sun)? Could it end up tearing itself loose from the planet?

item.title: Why it didn't cause an end of the world/Universe yet? How about this:
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps to explain that plothole, you could write a recovery log that shows that the inventor experienced the beginning of the doombubble from inside, it grew around him and only reached fatal levels after he was already completly, or at least mostly, inside; and at the time it was recovered, the inventor was running on it very dehydrated and suffering from radiation sickness, and died only moments after the recovery team found him; then something similar happened to some of the researchers, the research site, or whatever was around the treadmill at the time, got destroyed again, but this time the surviving researchers managed to figure out some preliminary containment procedures that allowed the knowledge they have today to be developed?

Do the numbers allow for the doombubble to grow around someone that is close enough without harming them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the article you directly quoted, and I'll bold for emphasis:

Safe-class objects are those anomalies that are either sufficiently understood that they are now completely and reliably contained on a permanent basis or otherwise do not trigger their anomalous effects unless intentionally activated.

We understand exactly what this does. It's predictable. It will never, ever, change. You probably had goddamn meetings and Ethics Committee hearings in-universe over this thing because we know exactly what it does and how it needs to be contained.

And that's all you need for safe.

Sure, you need to constantly keep it running. You need to have a mechanic on hand if it breaks. But it's predictable. We can completely and reliably contain it on a permanent basis. It's safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, I have to go with Mader Levap here.

I LOVE the idea behind this. But it is a logical paradox in existence. HOW did the Foundation come to control it? Unless SCPF was present at its creation, there is no way to get there in time and control it before it destroys us all.

As for the object class:

Safe-class objects are those anomalies that are either sufficiently understood that they are now completely and reliably contained on a permanent basis or otherwise do not trigger their anomalous effects unless intentionally activated.

This Scip requires constant intervention and attention. If not used, EVERYTHING dies.

Explain its existence in Foundation custody (even "we built it for some stupid reason"), and I'll consider rescinding my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object classes are based on difficulty and complexity of containment, not potential danger. A button that if pressed destroys the world would be Safe, because all you'd need to do would be to chuck it into a locker and put a bunch of locks on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, but -1. Reasons:
1. Safe? Safe?? Leave it alone for sufficiently long time (but very short on human timescale) and it destroys universe. What it is if not Keter?
2. Bigass plothole: if leaving this thing alone for even few hours makes it impossible to stop it, then our universe should be doomed already. Why? After creation of device, someone should notice it's effect, Foundation must be called, SCP safed, figured out… this takes a lot of time, especially initial period from creation to discovery.

So nope, I can't buy it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, this is scary. This should be Euclid, if not Keter. Here's why: Some nukes Alaska. 6 hours later, expanding bubble of death, which is impossible to ever shut off again because it instantly kills anything that approaches it. A few years later: the entire earth has been consumed, and every living thing on earth is dead.
This should be Keter for the same reason SCP-871 is Keter. While containment may be easy, it relies on constant human intervention, with end-of-the world scale consequences if that containment is removed even for a very small amount of time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this was created quite some time ago, and I'm not sure if this issue has been mentioned already, but I'm -really- curious:

How on Earth did we OBSERVE [it wasn't written as speculation, it seemed to be written as if it was an observed effect] the effect of walking across the threshold when the time differential was particularly high, without the world ending? If it basically causes people to die when the time differential is too high, for reasons explained in the article, how did we get someone to run on it and reverse the effect?

And, I wouldn't mind seeing some elaboration of the particularly catastrophic possible effects of leaving the treadmill alone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is a prime example of why having two different classes (one for containment, another for danger) would benefit the site. Sure, all you have to do to keep it contained is to run on it for a while everyday. But it it breaches containment… That's an XK class, with a possible YK (destruction of universe, or at least a radical reconfiguration of the universe) over the horizon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: dat length!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agh. I truly love this. The physics implications are awesome. Drewbear, i wish i could +1 your description of the effects on photons crossing the bubble a page back. It got my mind thinking. There's some interesting questions about the effects of heat within the bubble. Thermodynamics is still hppening in there, so everything is producing heat at an accelerated rate. Couple that with entropy dictating that the organization must ultimately decay, you could get the buble to define a region of intense heat relatively quickly. But then that heat would also be producing blackbody radiation, contributing to the runaway energy pulse going outside the field.
Alternatively, depending on the mechanics of the boundary (I'm kind of guessing here, since the effects of a temporal boundary are ill defined.) it's quite possible that the interior would register the (from it's perspective) lower thermal activity outside as a temperature gradient. Thermodynamically, this would induce heat to pass outside the boundary quite quickly, once the dilation became severe enough. But since any particle motion would be slowed down to our time scale, it wouldn't serve to actuall provide much heat, meaning that the gradient wouldn't change from heating up the outside, only from cooling the inside. So you could actually get a massive freeze instead, as all the heat is sucked away.
There's also an interesting question about how particles passing the boundary slow. It could just be that, BAM, they slow to the equivalent in the new time frame. But, alternatively, they could have to lose their energy after crossing the boundary (this seems more consistent with the photon behavior.) At first, this could mean waves of heat being emitted from the boundary, as particles bleed off energy through collisions and heating the surroundings. But at a certain point, the differential will be large enough that soem masses cross the boundary at speeds greater than the speed of light. This means either A) they would become tachyons (kind of boring, since tachyons are purely theoretical, so we can;t draw conclusions.) or B) They would have to immediately lose that energy to get below the speed of light. There's a phenomenon for this already, Cherenkov radiation, which causes the blue glow of nuclear reactors. This could mean that there would be a high energy light front fromm the boundary at that point.

Okay, one last thought. When light enters the field of the running treadmil (not bubble) it increases in frequency. But when it leaves the ara of effect, it would revert to its original spectrum. But in thje effects interior, it behaves with the properties of the modified frequency. Thus, at a certain value of X, you're going to have visible light enter the field, become X-rays, pass through the subject, exit the field, and become visible again. Thus you would get a false-color x-ray image of the subject and anything else in the effect visible as normal light! You could see their bones cast shadows whiule light passes right through! Heck, at certain ratios, the whole assemblage would turn invisible, as the radiation frequencies that view everything involved as transparent!

You made my brain happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What they said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember that the time differential works in opposite directions depending on whether it's the bubble vs running on it. If the bubble has formed and you're inside it, then yes, the outside world looks like it's moving slower than you. However, if you're running on the treadmill, then you're experiencing a slowdown and the outside environment appears to be moving faster than you. So if you were running @ 5 kph, you would only experience 1 minute, both mentally and physically, but the outside world had 2 hours pass. You're only as tired as if you'd been on the treadmill 1 minute. Incidentally, this means that, from the perspective of outside observors, you hovered in midair at some point as you switched feet while running.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So just to be clear, after entering the area of effect, the outside world would appear to be moving slower, correct? Or does just the effects of time happen in the area of effect? I like this story, but my head is having a hard time getting through the time bubble. :I

For example, if you spent one minute running at 5 km/h, would you have 2 hours of time to walk and think to yourself, or would you register only 1 minute but be as tired as if you walked for 2 hours?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't. Hence why they always have someone using it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once you've left the thing alone for a few hours, how do you cross the transition between normal time and treadmill time without killing yourself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instead of "upshift" and "downshift," I would prefer it if you used the proper scientific terms, which are blueshift and redshift, respectively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Math.
Stahp.

Anyways, +1. This is incredibly well-done, and although I had to read the discussion to understand just what the heck was going on, it was in a good way.

I'd be interested to see what would happen if this was document-dropped lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Psych major, actually, although I dearly love math and science and was 2 years into a second degree for Electrical Engineering degree before the money ran out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Drewbear, are you a mathematics or physics major perchance? I only ask because I'm in a fairly basic physics class right now, and I have just enough comprehension to really get just how out of my league your idea is.

On an SCP related note, supar good, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was an enjoyable read, even if th physics were a bit over my head.

I kinda want to put a model airplane on it though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, even with the table, I was a bit baffled by what exactly the tread mill did (though that's definitely not the author's fault; I just suck mightily at maths). I'm upvoting this almost solely for the explanation in the discussion, which made things a whole lot clearer AND a whole lot more horrifying too.

Though I have to say, the tables were very, very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just too dangerous to be interesting. Make the effect develop more slowly, and have it ramp down gradually with distance rather than cutting off all at once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An SCP that, while seemingly mundane (for an SCP), has a chance of giving reality a heart attack. I like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The current list of staff, mods and admins is at Guide to Newbies if you ever need to double check. That's also why it's important to leave the dreary "don't do that" stuff to the folks on that list so that there's no confusion.

(no one actually did anything wrong here just riding my shiny new hobbyhorse around and being obnoxious)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, except for the fact that a multiple series of Big Bangs would start occuring somewhere around 6 hours, occuring more and more rapidly. I imagine that having even one brand new universe explode within the heart of the Sun wouldn't be too healthy for it, much less a quintillion of them.

Keep in mind that after 24 hours, time within the sphere would be passing roughly 5.2 x 1069 times faster than outside. To give you a sense of scale, our universe is only roughly 4.336 x 1017 seconds old. Just within the 1 second that occurs at exactly 24 hours after the sphere began, enough time passes within the sphere for something to become 1.2 x 1052 times older than our universe. I imagine that's enough time for at least a few new universes to have spontaneously erupted, lived and decayed within that single second. And that's totally disregarding all the universes that existed within the preceeding 24 hours.

And light does still pass through that barrier. But any light passing from the inside to the outside is going to drastically upshift in energy. As in, a single photon passing through the barrier at that time differential would upshift enough that it would instantaneously acquire enough mass (energy can be converted to mass, remember) to become a massive pinpoint black hole. And that's just a single photon. Imagine what the rushing energy of a Big Bang would be like, hitting that threshold.

I… wow, I actually never thoroughly thought about what the exterior effects would be like if that much time was allowed to pass. Imagine a shell of supermassive black holes which are constantly getting exponentially more massive as they're fed by the influx of energy from the Big Bang engine at their core. I'm not sure how long it would take, but eventually (and I suspect in far less than a year), the Schwarzchild radius would be large enough to encompass the entirety of our universe, which would effectively mean the total destruction of everything. In less than a year (or so).

Wow. I knew this was dangerous, but I honestly didn't think far enough out to realize that it was literally "destroy the universe" dangerous. Damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sounds like a perfect case for the foundation's old motto: INTO THE FIERY ORB WITH YE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hands-down the most truly scientific sounding article I've seen in a long time. Well written and an interesting concept.

But I got an idea from Bland's comment, and it won't quit bugging me. Why do you need a human runner? Couldn't you just take that moped (or some more elgant, purpose-built machine) and rig it to run to match the desired setting permanently? You would still need the power redundancy (in fact, even more so, to power the auto-runner device) and the speed mechanic on stand-by, but it removes the risk of human error from the runner. No chance of a flywheel device tripping on a loose shoelace after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Salman Corbette has guru status as well. And Gene R, for obvious reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agree with Euclid…. or even Keter. It seems like it's well contained because yeah sure, one person can run it for 15 days at a time or whatever, but it's like one moderately serious disaster or oversight away from taking over the world. It has to be on ALL the time and it has to have a mechanic available ALL of the time and if you leave it alone for just an hour, assuming I read the chart correctly, things are moving almost 10,000 times slower inside the bubble. Essentially at that point, no one can run on it anymore (or actually, they wouldn't have been able to long before an hour) because their heart will probably explode the nanosecond they try to cross into it. So basically after like a half an hour of disuse because some asshole locked his keys in his office or Hurricane Clusterfuck killed everyone who was supposed to watch it, everyone in the world and eventually everything in existence will die as this thing crawls along 0.5cm/s. If anyone fucks up ever beyond a very short delay, we're all dead.

Safe? Really?

Also… I can't fathom a situation where this wouldn't have killed everyone already unless 1) The Foundation created it, and 2) They immediately knew its properties and had the resources to enact the containment procedures like OMG RIGHT NOW. I upvoted because I do like it but that's a hell of a plot gap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think Safe is appropriate. We know how to contain it. Euclid would imply that every now and then it does something we didn't expect even though all Secure Containment Procedures were followed.

For things that are otherwise easily contained to be Euclid they need to have some kind of mind of their own to try to think their way out of containment or just plain respond to containment in unpredictable ways.

For some reason, the fact that this thing is in Alaska and has a doom bubble that wastes things away really makes me imagine a connection to SCP-804 :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah - Karma has ZERO to do with SCP-Wiki; it's all about wikidot general stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because you have the maxed out karma. Aside from you, the only people I've seen with that are SS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Top speed of most models seems to be about 12 MPH. Still, with the acceleration rate, that's pretty damned significant.

Why do people keep thinking I'm with the SS? I'm tempted to ask Bright if I can use the a forum signature that says "No, I'm not Senior Staff! You can tell me to go to hell about anything!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been waffling on the Safe/Euclid thing. On one hand, no, you can't just stick it in a box and forget about it. On the other hand, its properties are well known and the containment procedures are fairly straight-forward. I initially had it as Euclid, but the class definitions in the navbar seem to indicate that it should be Safe. I'm cool with it either way, so if Senior Staff say it ought to be one way vs the other, feel absolutely free to change it.

I hadn't thought about the time dilation combo much, but now that I am, here's Word of God: since the treadmill isn't affected by the Doom Bubble (because I didn't want it decaying to dust), then someone who gets on it and starts using it won't be either; they're syncing with the treadmill, not the bubble.

As for the moped, remember that the dilation is based on the speed setting of the treadmill, not how fast you're actually going on it. I doubt that an exercise treadmill is programmed to go any faster than 10-12 mph.

Also, remember that the treadmill only slows down when it's powered and in use. If the outside power source fails, then the relativistic runner immediately snaps back to normal speed. Just in case this happens, one of those triple-redundancy power supplies is an uninterruptible power supply physical lashed to the treadmill, so even if the Doom Bubble starts to form, there's power available to start up the treadmill again.

And I thought the mental image of this jogger suspended in mid-air while running was a good one, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The charts help a LOT.

This is Euclid, by the way. You have to have a mechanic and runners on standby at all times or else the field will expand. If it expands too much no one can get in the sphere to stop it. Theoretically, even if they DO get inside, the time effect…

Actually, which way does that work? You get inside the sphere alive (no easy feat) and start running. The sphere retracts but the inside is now moving FASTER so getting on and running is no problem; you start running but the sphere on the outside retracts at the outside speed. But you're slowed down by the running and the sphere is trying to speed you up… Do they subtract, multiply, or just cancel each other out?

My solution is to rig a moped to it and get it up to 50 MPH. Sure it would flash-fry the rider using background infra-red radiation, but even a second or two would probably take it well beyond the lifecycle of the solar system - maybe even the galaxy! (Whee! Bignums!)

I do like the idea that the faster you go on the treadmill the slower it appears you to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, so long as they were still kept in the darkened room, they wouldn't see anything. If they ran fast enough, they might see an dim ambient reddish light as the heat in the room upshifted to visible. If the room was lighted, well, that's what happened to the D-class in Addendum A. Also, the lead lining of the room it to minimize outside electromagnetic waves/radiation, especially higher energy, so as to minimize the runner's exposure to dangerous levels of radiation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They definitely help. Especially when you're getting into numbers like e, it can be hard to keep those numbers under control. Also, it helped clearly describe the exact dilation of time.

It also makes me wonder what the user would see as he/she was running.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Coming back after a couple of days, I see the charts you've added. Somewhat dry, but very illuminating. So the faster your rate, the slower you run. Any further confusion is solely my own and amounts to: do they need to run for 55 minutes of their time or ours? Well, I guess it doesn't matter either way, since if you can get 15 days' worth of running from a minute at 10 kph (6 mph, a nice jogging speed), then this thing's extremely easy to keep under control. Which is fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That really helps a lot. Also like Psyshade said, the tables you added keep the clinical tone intact and make things a lot clearer for readers like us.

Too bad I can't upvote you twice :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of months ago I spent several hours trying to get my table in the sandbox done the way I wanted to be, and my conclusion is that the table available in the quick buttons simply can't do pretty anything I wanted, so this is what I did:

AAAA BBBB
AAAA1 BBBB1
AAAA2 BBBB2
AAAA3 BBBB3
And here's the code
Fold the code
 [[table style="border-collapse:collapse;"]] [[row]] [[cell style="font-weight:bold; text-align: center; border: 1px solid black; background-color: silver; width: 50%;"]] AAAA [[/cell]] [[cell style="font-weight:bold; text-align: center; border: 1px solid black; background-color: silver;"]] BBBB [[/cell]] [[/row]] [[row]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] AAAA1 [[/cell]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] BBBB1 [[/cell]] [[/row]] [[row]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] AAAA2 [[/cell]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] BBBB2 [[/cell]] [[/row]] [[row]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] AAAA3 [[/cell]] [[cell style="text-align: center; border: 1px solid black"]] BBBB3 [[/cell]] [[/row]] [[/table]]

Let me know if you have any questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think they help. Being not so mathly inclined, I was not sure what the differential was until you explained it here. The tables keep the clinical tone intact while expressing the effect clearly to readers; I could see this report in the hands of a researcher with (or without) the tables.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added some charts to help those having difficulty getting their heads around the math. Do they help or do they detract?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mathematical explanation, for me, a math derp, is just a bonus. You can understand what this thing is doing without the 'complicated' explanation, so to me it isn't really a problem (it go faster, time go faster).

As much as I'm not a fan of the concept the writing is good and it's a believable SCP, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did you get that shading done? I wanted to do that for a different one of my SCP items, but couldn't figure out how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They figure out it was e by noting that the "faster" someone ran on the treadmill, the slower they appeared to the outside world. So they stuck someone w/ a stop-watch on the treadmill at 1 km/h for something like 10 minutes and saw that the differential was exactly e. Then they tested it the same way at 2 km/h, 3 km/h, etc.

As for why I chose e, I wanted to use one of the "special" constants, but not pi. And what better number for an exponentiating number than e? Besides, I have no idea what the ASCII code is for phi. :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the math lesson, I'm really confused with the concept of this SCP until you explain it in your quick math lesson.

I can see the mathematical/physics approach you're trying and as far as I know, this is the most scientifically-written SCP I've ever read. However, the problem with this approach is that most of us get lost when trying to understand what these equations really mean.

Yes, I know it's an SCP Foundation report, as an "in-character" aspect everyone who's reading this report should be able to fully understand what this SCP really does. However, this also makes most of us normal people very hard to understand (or even ignore) the real threat of this SCP. It doesn't only "slow" time, it "slows" time in a very terrifying rate if you turn its speed setting up.

Perhaps a table would help people to understand your concept better than just plainly explain it in mathematics equations?

Speed Setting of SCP-1979 Time Differential (per second)
10 km/h (6.2 mph) 367 Hours
15 km/h (9.3 mph) 908 Hours
20 km/h (12.4 mph) 134766 Hours

Hope I got all these numbers right, and if you decide to add a table, that will be a lot easier for us to understand your main concept.

P.S. Sorry for the multiple editing, this new table format I've just learned is very tricky to use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the few things on this site to genuinely frighten me. Anything that screws with the passage rate of time is a scary thing. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only question I have about it is how it was determined to be e and not something else, like the golden ratio? Did you just pick or was there a reason behind it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
tl;dr

Rating-wise, this article is doing quite well, but…

If you have to put that much of an explanation in the discussion page (I feel, anyway) there's a problem.

Maybe a graph or something on the article page.

edit: Actually, your "quick" math lesson is almost exactly as long as the Description in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first time i've seen someone so concisely tackle the physical and electromagnetic effects of crossing the threshold of a time bubble. I am +1ing this so hard it'll walk with a limp tomorrow!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That does seem to have confused some people both here and in chat, so let me try to explain it a bit for you. The reason they keep it running for 55 minutes, even though it doesn't activate until it's been unused for 55, is that they don't want to come anywhere near that 30 minute mark. Better to have a much higher margin of error. And yes, you'd need a constant stream of people on it. Luckily, the time dilation means that one person running on it could last for a long, long time. In the addendum, I mentioned that the D-class was only running on the treadmill for one minute of her time, but was on it for (a little over) 15 days our time.

Quick math lesson:

e is an irrational number, which means that it never ends and never repeats, like pi. However, for the purposes of this article, we can round it to 2.71828.

The equation that it uses to determine the time differential when running on it is e-x, which can also be expressed as saying that for every 1 second (or minute) the runner experiences, the outside observer sees ex seconds (or minutes).

At 10 km/h (~6.2 mph), the runner experiences 1 minute, but we experience e10 minutes, which is roughly 22026 minutes. 22026 minutes divided by 60 makes roughly 367 hours. 367 hours dived by 24 makes a little over 15 days.

On the other side, let's say that the Doom Bubble has formed. It reaches 1 meter in radius after 200 seconds (3 minutes, 20 seconds) and time on the inside is running e.37 * 1 times faster, roughly 1.44 times faster. Not too bad yet. It reaches 5 meters in radius after 1000 seconds (16 minutes, 40 seconds) and time on the inside is running e.37 * 5 times faster, roughly 6.36 times faster. Oops, we're already past the danger zone. It reaches 10 meters after 2000 seconds (33 minutes, 20 seconds) and time on the inside is running e.37 * 10 times faster, roughly 40.45 times faster. Crap, that's adding up. It reaches 100 meters after 20,000 seconds (5 hours, 33 minutes, 20 seconds). Time is running e.37 * 100 times faster, roughly 11.7 * 1015 times faster.

If that last number is a little too large to get your head around, let me put it like this: at that distance/rate, for every second that passes for us, 371 million years pass inside. And it only gets ever worse, ever faster, the larger the sphere gets. So yeah. Doom bubble.

P.S. For those of you interested, it would reach "the age of our universe for every second on the outside" somewhere between 133 and 134 meters in radius. EDIT: Derp, it's actually somewhere between 109 & 110. I had misplaced a decimal originally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also holding back an upvote because of the confusing mathematics going on here. I get that the major danger is from crossing the time dilation field, and I like that, but there's still something bugging me.

If it takes 30 minutes for the field to activate, why does it need to be used for 55? It seems like you'd have a constant stream of personnel running on this thing, and because I can't wrap my head around the math, I have no idea if they're running for days or seconds on their end. Perhaps a suggestion of what speed to run at is in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's at least Euclid for this reason. I would almost think it's Keter, since the field WILL expand if it's left alone, but I may be overracting with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you mean that it should have to be plugged in for it to start fucking shit up for the outside world, well, where's the fun in that?

And that, good sir, is why this is an SCP subject instead of an entry on the Log of Anomalous Items!
